Paul opens this letter not with casual greeting but with a confession that arrests your attention: everything flowing from his pen comes not from his own ambition but "by the will of God." Then, without transition, he bursts into blessing. Before offering theology or instruction, he calls the Ephesian church to join him in praising God for spiritual blessings already given—not someday, not when you achieve something, but now, in Christ. This is not sentiment. Paul is declaring that the moment you believed, you inherited a reality so complete that he cannot restrain himself from doxology. The blessings are not potential or conditional. They are "in Christ with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly realms." Your status before God is already settled, not because you deserved it or earned it, but because God chose you before time existed.

What Paul describes next should shake you: God chose you before the foundation of the world. He predestined you for adoption. He did this according to the good pleasure of His will, not because He saw your future faithfulness or predicted your choice. This is not negotiable biblical teaching, however difficult it feels. And yet Paul frames it not as threatening but as the ground of your deepest comfort. God's sovereign choice of you means your redemption is not fragile, dependent on your performance, or reversible. Your forgiveness through Christ's blood is already yours. Your status as God's child is not provisional. You are sealed by the Holy Spirit—marked as belonging to God, guaranteed your inheritance, protected until the day you fully receive what He has promised. This is the foundation Paul stands on, and he cannot praise God without making it explicit.

The second half of the chapter shifts to prayer, but not away from this theme. Paul asks that your heart's eyes be opened to know what you already possess spiritually but have perhaps not fully grasped. He prays that you would understand the hope of your calling, the riches of your inheritance, and the incomparable power at work for you—the same power that raised Christ from the dead and seated Him "far above all rule and authority, power and dominion" in the heavenly realms. This power is not distant or abstract. It is working on your behalf right now. And Christ's supremacy is absolute and permanent, extending not only over the present age but into the one to come. When Paul concludes that God made Christ head over everything for the church, He is telling you that the entire cosmos is organized for your welfare. The church—you—is Christ's body, the vessel through which His fullness expresses itself into all creation.

Begin reading Ephesians with this settled reality burning in your mind: your standing before God is not based on what you will do but on what God has done, and it cannot be shaken. The enemy wants you anxious about your status, conditional about your acceptance, perpetually performing for divine approval. Paul confronts that lie at the very start. You are chosen. You are redeemed. You are sealed. You are His. Everything else in this letter builds on that foundation. Rather than live in that truth, many of us live as if we still had to earn it. Ask God to open the eyes of your heart to see what He has already given you.
